sound_type: continuous electric buzz -- sustained high-pitched drone produced by wing stridulation, NOT rhythmic chirps like cricket (87) but a continuous unbroken buzz that sounds almost electrical, E=4, SECOND CONSECUTIVE INSECT, back-to-back Orthoptera
ecosystem: Shrub/meadow. Shrubs, hedgerows, meadow edges, low trees. Arboreal and shrub-dwelling. Elevated calling positions. Contrasts with ground-dwelling cricket (87).
season: Summer (peak calling on warm nights). Active late spring through early fall.

species_pair.pair_type: Back-to-back Orthoptera (87-88). Same mechanism (wing stridulation), different result (rhythmic chirp vs continuous buzz). Ground vs shrub. Two families exploring one mechanism.

coordinates.description: Reference for shrub/meadow habitat in the Puget Sound lowlands, Washington. Katydid buzz audible in shrubs and meadow edges throughout the region.

taxonomy_note.description: SECOND CONSECUTIVE INSECT (87-88). Back-to-back Orthoptera. Tettigoniidae NEW FAMILY (39th). Same order Orthoptera as cricket (87). Back-to-back new families in the same order. Gryllidae (38th) and Tettigoniidae (39th). Family-level entry.
taxonomy_note.infraclass_note: Insecta uses subclass Pterygota (winged insects). Not tracked at infraclass level.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Tettigoniidae from Greek tettix/tettigonia ('cicada-like'), referring to the buzzing sound. Also known as bush-crickets or long-horned grasshoppers.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Family-level entry. Multiple genera contribute: Scudderia (bush katydids), Microcentrum (broad-winged katydids), Conocephalus (meadow katydids).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Orthoptera -- same order as cricket (87). Second consecutive Orthoptera entry. Back-to-back Orthoptera.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Insecta -- second insect entry (after cricket at 87). Fourth class in the engine (Aves, Mammalia, Amphibia, Insecta).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Tettigoniidae's FIRST entry at degree 88. NEW FAMILY (39th). Back-to-back new families with Gryllidae (38th at degree 87).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Approximately 5,000-20,000 Hz. Higher than crickets (3,000-5,000 Hz). Some species near or above upper limit of human hearing.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Species-dependent. Typically 6,000-12,000 Hz. High-pitched buzz dominates upper frequency range.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. STRIDULATION BUZZ. Sustained, continuous drone without discrete pulses.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual buzz: sustained for seconds to minutes without interruption. Calling bouts: hours-long on warm summer nights.
song_metrics.note_structure: Continuous buzz -- sustained wing-stroke oscillation producing an unbroken drone. No discrete chirps or pulses. Some species produce 'katy-DID, katy-DIDN'T' phrase but many produce sustained buzz.
song_metrics.note_types: Calling buzz (primary advertisement, sustained), aggressive buzz (modified frequency/intensity during male-male encounters), courtship buzz (modified near female).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Continuous, sustained drone. No gaps. No rhythmic pulsing. The buzz fills the frequency band without interruption. Multiple katydids calling simultaneously create overlapping, continuous drone across the shrub canopy.
song_metrics.timbre: Electric, droning, high-pitched. Sounds more like a power line or electronic oscillator than a biological sound. Sharp, buzzy. Distinct from cricket's bright, metallic chirp.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Individual calls audible at 15-30 meters. Continuous nature fills soundscape without gaps. Multiple individuals produce overlapping sustained drone. E=4 reflects persistent, space-filling quality.
song_metrics.tempo: N/A -- continuous buzz, not rhythmic. No discrete tempo. The buzz is sustained without temporal structure.
song_metrics.context: BREEDING CALL: males stridulate from elevated positions in shrubs to attract females. Night calling from shrub canopy. Summer. Peaks on warm nights. Nocturnal.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Multiple species contribute. Different species have different frequency ranges and call patterns. PNW species include fork-tailed bush katydid and meadow katydids.
song_metrics.key_property: CONTINUOUS ELECTRIC BUZZ -- the 89th acoustic element. Second insect sound. Sustained stridulation drone. NOT rhythmic like cricket (87) but CONTINUOUS. High-frequency (5,000-20,000 Hz). Sounds electric. E=4 through persistent space-filling. Back-to-back Orthoptera stridulation (87-88).

physical_description.overall: Family-level entry. Bush katydids (Scudderia spp.): 30-50 mm, bright green, elongated body, very long antennae (often longer than body), leaf-like wings. Broad-winged katydids (Microcentrum spp.): 40-65 mm, green, rounded leaf-like wings. Meadow katydids (Conocephalus spp.): 15-25 mm, green or brown, slender.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 88: (1) LEAF-LIKE APPEARANCE -- green, flat, wing veins resemble leaf veins. (2) VERY LONG ANTENNAE -- much longer than body (longer than cricket antennae). (3) STRIDULATION -- males rub forewings to produce continuous buzz. (4) ARBOREAL -- found in shrubs and trees, not ground. (5) SOUND -- most often detected by the buzz, not by sight.
physical_description.skin_detail: EXOSKELETON: Chitinous exoskeleton. Primarily green (most species), some brown or mixed. Wings: leaf-like, with venation patterns mimicking leaf structure. Forewings modified for stridulation (file and scraper).
physical_description.field_id: Most often detected by SOUND -- the continuous high-pitched buzz on summer nights. Remarkably leaf-like in appearance. Green, flat, leaf-vein wing patterns. Very long antennae. Arboreal -- in shrubs and trees, not on the ground like crickets. Heard far more than seen.

nodes.title: Back-to-Back Orthoptera (87-88)
nodes.description: Second consecutive Orthoptera. Cricket (87) and katydid (88). Same mechanism (stridulation), different result (chirp vs buzz). Like Anatidae (58-59). Ground vs shrub. Two families exploring one mechanism.

nodes.title: Continuous Electric Buzz -- 89th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Sustained drone. Wing stridulation. Sounds electric. High-pitched (5,000-20,000 Hz). E=4. Fills shrub canopy. Continuous without gaps. Distinct from cricket's rhythmic chirp.

nodes.title: Tettigoniidae -- 39th Family -- Same Order, New Family
nodes.description: NEW FAMILY. Bush-crickets / katydids. 39th family. Same order Orthoptera as Gryllidae (87). Back-to-back new families in the same order.

nodes.title: Second Consecutive Insect (87-88)
nodes.description: Second insect in the engine. Back-to-back insects. Both Orthoptera. Both stridulation. Both E=4. Different families, different habitats, different results.

nodes.title: Stridulation: Chirp vs Buzz
nodes.description: Same mechanism (wing rubbing), different result. Cricket = discrete chirps, rhythmic. Katydid = continuous drone, no gaps. Two expressions of one mechanism in consecutive degrees.

nodes.title: Three Consecutive Non-Mammalian Entries (86-87-88)
nodes.description: Pacific Treefrog (86, Amphibia), cricket (87, Insecta), katydid (88, Insecta). Three consecutive non-mammalian entries. Longest non-mammalian streak since bird era (0-60).

nodes.title: Shrub/Meadow Habitat -- Arboreal Calling
nodes.description: Arboreal/shrub-dwelling. Elevated calling position in shrub canopy. Contrasts with ground-dwelling cricket (87). Meadow shared with Snowshoe Hare (73). Vertical niche separation.

nodes.title: The Kimya Dawson Parallel
nodes.description: Both continuous and stripped-down. Katydid buzzes all night without interruption. Dawson strums entire songs without embellishment. Both minimal in structure but persistent in presence. Persistence, not force.

nodes.title: Twenty-Eighth Consecutive Original CSV
nodes.description: Twenty-eight consecutive original CSV species. No substitution. Original placement at degree 88.

nodes.title: Bat-Katydid Frequency Arms Race
nodes.description: Bats are primary nocturnal predators. Echolocation detects katydid calls. Some katydids evolved to shift call frequency above bat echolocation range. A frequency arms race between predator and prey.

cross_references.relationship: BACK-TO-BACK ORTHOPTERA. Same mechanism (stridulation), different result (chirp vs buzz). Gryllidae (87) and Tettigoniidae (88). Ground vs shrub. Rhythmic vs continuous. Two families exploring one mechanism.
THREE CONSECUTIVE NON-MAMMALIAN ENTRIES (86-87-88). Treefrog (Amphibia), cricket (Insecta), katydid (Insecta). Three consecutive chorusing/buzzing species. The chorus zone extends.
ELECTRIC BUZZ QUALITY SHARED. Varied Thrush's ethereal buzzy whistle has electric quality similar to katydid's drone. Different phyla, similar timbral impression.
CONTINUOUS SOUND. Both produce continuous, sustained sounds. Beaver gnaws without stopping; katydid buzzes without stopping. Both fill soundscapes through persistence.
MEADOW SHARED. Both inhabit meadow edges and shrub habitats. Katydid calls from shrub canopy above where hare forages below. Vertical niche separation.
